abstract: This preregistered field experiment tested whether reducing the visibility of political elites in X (Twitter) feeds improves users' belief accuracy. Using a custom mobile X client (Twilly), the authors randomly assigned 1,214 U.S. participants to a control condition, an Elite Blocking condition (hiding posts authored by political elites), or an Elite Scrubbing condition (additionally hiding posts mentioning elites) over a one-month period in late 2024. Both interventions reduced exposure to and resharing of low-credibility and highly partisan content, and produced significant improvements in factual knowledge and reductions in misinformation susceptibility, while leaving affective polarization, media trust, and related attitudes largely unchanged.
key_claims: Elite-centered exposure on social media causally shapes belief accuracy and misinformation susceptibility, not merely individual traits like partisanship or inattention.
Selectively suppressing elite visibility can improve epistemic outcomes without broadly suppressing political content or requiring users to disengage from politics.
Elite-referential discourse (posts mentioning elites) contributes additional amplification of low-credibility content beyond elite-authored posts themselves.
Misinformation should be reframed as a problem of exposure architecture rather than only individual belief or motivation.
Platform-independent client-side interventions are a feasible and causally informative method for studying social media effects amid restricted API access.
methods: Preregistered field experiment (September–December 2024) with a baseline (N=1,214) and endline (N=648) survey separated by a one-month intervention period.
Custom mobile X client ('Twilly') that filtered users' live timelines client-side while preserving follow networks and ordinary scrolling.
Three-arm random assignment: Control (unaltered feed), Elite Blocking (hide elite-authored posts), and Elite Scrubbing (hide elite-authored and elite-mentioning posts).

Elite accounts identified using ideology estimates from Barberá (2015) and Mukerjee et al. (2022), updated for 2024; low-credibility content identified via domain ratings (Mosleh & Rand; Robertson et al.); partisan content defined as tweets with |ideology score| > 0.3.
Recruitment via CloudResearch of active U.S. X users; behavioral compliance monitored via app usage logs.

Linear regression models for survey outcomes (with demographic and baseline covariates) and engagement models with treatment × engagement-type interactions; 2SLS instrumental-variable analyses using random assignment as instrument for exposure.

Outcomes measured pre/post: political knowledge (national/international), text and multimodal misinformation susceptibility, confidence in misinformation judgments, affective polarization, political attitudes, media trust, and news avoidance.
findings: Both interventions reduced daily tweet volume substantially (e.g., from 586 to 350 tweets/day for left-leaning users under Elite Scrubbing) while leaving the share of political content roughly stable.
Exposure to low-credibility content dropped ~5.8–5.9 percentage points in viewed tweets under both interventions; partisan content viewed dropped ~13.7–14.0 percentage points.
Retweeting of partisan content fell sharply (~20 percentage points) under both conditions; retweeting of low-credibility content declined significantly under Elite Blocking (−6.79 pp).

Treatment effects concentrated in first-degree exposure (followed accounts) rather than second-degree exposure via linked domains; Elite Scrubbing produced larger reductions in low-credibility exposure than Elite Blocking (Δ ≈ 3.2 pp).
International news knowledge increased by 0.48 SD (Elite Blocking) and 0.32 SD (Elite Scrubbing); national news knowledge increased by ~0.24–0.28 SD.

Misinformation susceptibility decreased significantly across formats: political rumors (~−0.42 SD), multimodal misinformation (~−0.66 to −0.68 SD), and confidence in misinformation judgments (~−0.34 to −0.39 SD).
No significant effects detected on affective polarization, perceived partisan threat, social distance, political interest, institutional trust, media trust, or news avoidance.
Participants showed limited awareness of feed alterations: only 23% of treated participants noticed any change.
contributions: Provides causal evidence that elite visibility, distinct from broader political content exposure, drives misinformation susceptibility and low-credibility content sharing.
Disentangles elites-as-creators from elites-as-objects-of-attention by contrasting Elite Blocking and Elite Scrubbing conditions.
Demonstrates a platform-independent, client-side experimental methodology (Twilly) for manipulating live feeds without platform consent while holding follow networks fixed.
Identifies elite visibility as an actionable design lever for content moderation that improves epistemic outcomes without wholesale suppression of political content.
Reframes misinformation interventions from individual-level bottom-up approaches toward top-down structural changes in exposure architecture.
framing: The paper positions itself against bottom-up, individual-susceptibility accounts of misinformation (e.g., media literacy, inoculation, corrective prompts) and within a growing literature on top-down, structural and elite-driven misinformation dynamics. It contributes to platform-independent field experimentation on social media, building on and contrasting with prior work on feed re-ranking, unfollowing, and deactivation experiments by isolating elite-centered exposure as a distinct causal channel.
